sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 93, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 92 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 3.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't move the feet this time. The feet are still at rows 39-43, cols 9-13 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right). So SPACE only moves the feet down when they're in certain positions.
Let me think about this more carefully. The first SPACE moved the feet from rows 29-33 to rows 39-43 (down 10 rows). The second SPACE didn't move the feet. Maybe SPACE only works when the feet are in the arm lower area (rows 29-38).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. The feet were at rows 29-33 before the first SPACE, and they moved to rows 39-43. But the feet are now at rows 39-43, and SPACE didn't move them. So SPACE might only work when the feet are in a specific zone.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the goal is to have the feet at the initial positions:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
To get the left feet to rows 19-23, cols 19-23:
- Current: rows 39-43, cols 9-13
- Need to move up 20 rows and right 10 cols
To get the right feet to rows 54-58, cols 44-48:
- Current: rows 39-43, cols 44-48
- Need to move down 11 rows (already at correct cols)
But this would take many actions, and I'm already over budget.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to have the feet at the "hands" at rows 29-38, but at the correct columns. The hands are at cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right).
Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 9-13 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right).
To move the feet to the hands:
- Move up 4-14 rows (from 39-43 to 29-38)
- Move left feet right 5 cols (from 9-13 to 14-18)
- Move right feet right 5 cols (from 44-48 to 49-53)
But I can't move both feet right simultaneously, since LEFT moves left feet left and right feet right, and RIGHT moves left feet right and right feet left.
Wait, actually, let me reconsider. The feet move independently. When I press LEFT, the left feet move left and the right feet move right. When I press RIGHT, the left feet move right and the right feet move left. So:
- To move left feet right: press RIGHT
- To move right feet right: press LEFT
These are conflicting. I can't move both feet right with a single action.
But wait, the feet might be constrained by the body shape. If the left feet are at cols 9-13, pressing LEFT would try to move them left, but they might be blocked by the body boundary. Similarly, if the right feet are at cols 44-48, pressing RIGHT would try to move them left, but they might be blocked.
Let me try pressing LEFT to see if the right feet move right while the left feet stay in place (blocked).
Actually, I think I need to step back and think about this problem differently. I've spent 92 actions without completing the level, and I'm way over budget. Let me try to find a pattern or shortcut.
Looking at the initial state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet are at the center of the body (cols 19-23), and the right feet are at the bottom of the legs (rows 54-58).
Maybe the goal is to have the feet at the "hands" at rows 29-38, cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right). This would be a symmetric position at the ends of the arms.
Let me try to move the feet to this position:
1. Move left feet up from rows 39-43 to rows 29-38: 4-14 rows up
2. Move left feet right from cols 9-13 to cols 14-18: 5 cols right
3. Move right feet up from rows 39-43 to rows 29-38: 4-14 rows up
4. Move right feet right from cols 44-48 to cols 49-53: 5 cols right
To do this efficiently:
- Press UP 10 times to move feet to rows 29-33
- Press RIGHT 5 times to move left feet right (but this also moves right feet left)
- Press LEFT 5 times to move right feet right (but this also moves left feet left)
This would cancel out the horizontal movements. So I need a different approach.
Actually, maybe the feet can move horizontally independently. Let me test this by pressing RIGHT once and seeing if the left feet move right while the right feet stay in place (blocked).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 9-13 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right)
# Let me test if feet can move horizontally independently

# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
feet_before = []
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        feet_before.append((min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))
print(f"Before: {sorted(feet_before)}")

# Try RIGHT once
action(['RIGHT'])

# Check positions after
seg = current_frame.segmentation
feet_after = []
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        feet_after.append((min(rows), max(rows), min(cols), max(cols)))
print(f"After RIGHT: {sorted(feet_after)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
